raw_case:
Aberrant oviduct pathway following vacuum aspiration: A case report and literature review
We report the case of a patient addressed for abnormal uterine bleeding in the context of secondary infertility. Operative hysteroscopy under sedation showed multiple uterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome) and communication with the pelvic cavity suspecting uterine perforation. Combined laparoscopy showed that the right fallopian tube was incarcerated into the uterine wall. We conducted bilateral salpingectomy followed by uterine wall suture whose integrity was controlled by hysteroscopy. We highlight a rare, underdiagnosed complication of uterine perforation: oviduct incarceration into the uterine cavity.

Introduction

The incidence of uterine perforation after dilatation and curettage is less than 0.2% [1]. Subsequent complications of the vacuum aspiration leading to incarceration of an oviduct into the uterine cavity are rare and are limited to a few case reports [[2], [3], [4], [5]].We describe the case of an intrauterine fallopian tube incarceration found 4 years after the initial uterine perforation.

Case report

A 40-year-old woman presenting symptoms of abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) since a few months, was referred to our Fertility Clinic for secondary infertility lasting 1 year. The patient was gravida 1 para 1, she delivered vaginally 4 years ago. The patient experienced placental retention, which required an emergency vacuum curettage to remove residual trophoblastic material. She had no reports of chronic pelvic pain nor dysmenorrhea.Upon physical examination, the abdomen was nontender and painless, the gynecologic examination was painless as well, and no obvious bleeding was observed during the speculum exam.Transvaginal ultrasound showed a hyperechogenic thickened endometrial lining (doppler score 2) suggesting at first sight the presence of an isthmic endometrial polyp of 13 millimeters (Figs. 1A and B). Fundal lesions were also seen, suspicious of adenomyosis and intrauterine adhesions (Fig. 1, Fig. 1).Both assessments of saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS) and diagnostic hysteroscopy failed due a stenosis of the cervical orifice. Thus, a pelvic magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed which concluded to an endometrial polyp associated with a nodular subserosa fundic mass in communication with the uterine cavity, as well as a left hematosalpinx and suspicion of endometriotic lesions (Figs. 2A and B).The MRI protocol was subsequently discussed in a multidisciplinary meeting with fertility specialists and pelvic gynecology surgeons. As a result, an operative hysteroscopy for polyp excision and a concomitant laparoscopy for suspected hematosalpinx and endometriotic lesions were scheduled.The first step of the surgery was the operative hysteroscopy to identify and remove intracavitary lesions. Upon hysteroscope introduction, uterine cavity distention was impossible as the cavity shared multiple uterine adhesions evoking Asherman syndrome. While removing adhesions, parts of abdominal fat were visualized suggestive of uterine perforation. Per operative ultrasound also showed saline leak towards the abdominal cavity.Secondly, a laparoscopy was performed to identify the site of uterine perforation. Pelvic inspection showed an incarcerated right fallopian tube into the myometrial wall continuing into the uterine cavity (Figs. 3A and B). The incarcerated oviduct was removed (Figs. 3C and D), and bilateral salpingectomy was performed, due to the presence of a left hematosalpinx. No signs of endometriotic lesions were found during the laparoscopic inspection. The uterine wall defect was sutured using Vicryl 0 absorbable sutures. Finally, uterine wall integrity was confirmed by hysteroscopy.To summarize, both operative hysteroscopy and subsequent laparoscopy confirmed fallopian tube incarceration into the uterine cavity. Pathologic findings revealed chronic salpingitis lesions in the right oviduct and hydrosalpinx in the left oviduct. A 6-week postoperative diagnostic hysteroscopy showed normal endometrial lining as well as a small fundal septum (Fig. 4).

Discussion

The prevalence of abdominopelvic organ involvement during vacuum curettage is difficult to estimate as it is a rare occurrence that could go unrecognized. Intrauterine oviduct incarceration is a rare complication that can occur after procedures such as vacuum curettage, often performed for miscarriages, abortion or trophoblast remnants.In one case report, a patient experienced chronic abdominal pain after a first-trimester pregnancy abortion which required dilatation and vacuum aspiration. Both imaging and hysteroscopy combined to peroperative laparoscopy revealed the right fallopian tube had been iatrogenically pulled through the myometrium at the uterine fundus, resulting in its incarceration [2]. The patient had to undergo surgery to extract the fallopian tube from its fundal insertion site. In another case report, the diagnostic hysteroscopy was possible and showed indeed fallopian tube fimbriae within the uterine cavity [4].Fallopian tube incarceration remains a difficult diagnosis. In a case report published in 2011, suction curettage in the puerperium resulted in an oviduct incarceration misdiagnosed as placental residue [3].While pelvic ultrasound, when used alongside magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), can help clarify ambiguous cases, this type of complication can still go unrecognized. As shown in a case report published in 2018, fallopian tube incarceration was misidentified by MRI as a hydrosalpinx with a fimbriae lesion and an endometriotic nodule [5]. Typically, hysteroscopy and laparoscopy are the primary diagnostic tools, with ultimate confirmation provided by a pathology report.Conversely, uterine perforation also may contain bowels or the omentum as reported by Xholli and colleagues [6], who published a diagnostic and therapeutic guide for omental incarcerations.Such cases highlight that most postcurettage perforations go unnoticed, as the clinical manifestations related to fallopian tube or omental incarceration are nonspecific.While there is no proof that ultrasound-assisted curettage could diminish this type of complication, peroperative ultrasound guidance could be a valuable tool when available.If organ incarceration occurs, surgery is required to remove the entrapped organ and repair the uterine wall.

Conclusion

Intrauterine vacuum aspiration is a routine procedure that can be complicated by uterine perforation and abdomino-pelvic organ incarceration. This case report emphasizes, that even if rare, fallopian tube incarceration should be part of the differential diagnosis of thickened endometrium with or without AUB, in cases of history of vacuum aspiration.

Disclosures

During the preparation of this work the authors used ChatGPT in order to review and refine spelling and grammar. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

Patient consent

The patient has been informed about the purpose and the nature of this publication and has given both verbal and written consent for their medical information to be included in this article.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 40-year-old woman.
- **Chief Complaint:** Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) and secondary infertility for 1 year.
- **History of Present Illness:** AUB for several months; infertility for 1 year. History of placental retention after vaginal delivery 4 years ago, requiring emergency vacuum curettage.
- **Past Medical History:** None specified beyond the prior obstetric event.
- **Personal History:** Gravida 1 para 1; vaginal delivery 4 years ago. No chronic pelvic pain or dysmenorrhea.
- **Family History:** Not mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Abdomen and gynecological examinations were nontender and painless. Speculum exam showed no active bleeding.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Transvaginal ultrasound showed hyperechogenic thickened endometrial lining suspected as an endometrial polyp, with fundal lesions suggestive of adenomyosis and intrauterine adhesions.
  - Saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS) and diagnostic hysteroscopy failed due to cervical stenosis.
  - Pelvic MRI identified an endometrial polyp, a nodular subserosa fundic mass communicating with the uterine cavity, left hematosalpinx, and suspected endometriotic lesions.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Differential Diagnoses:**
  1. **Endometrial Polyp:** Suggested by imaging findings, but could not be directly visualized during SIS or hysteroscopy.
  2. **Adenomyosis:** Suspected based on the fundal lesions seen on ultrasound.
  3. **Intrauterine Adhesions (Asherman Syndrome):** Multiple uterine adhesions noted during operative hysteroscopy.
  4. **Uterine Perforation with Organ Incarceration (Tubal Incarceration):** Considered upon observing abdominal fat during hysteroscopy and detecting saline leakage on ultrasound.
  5. **Endometriosis:** Initial suspicion based on imaging findings, later disproven during laparoscopy.

- **Rationale for Additional Steps:**
  - **Hysteroscopy and Laparoscopy:** Used to directly evaluate the uterine cavity and pelvic structures. Confirmed uterine perforation, fallopian tube incarceration, and intrauterine adhesions.
  - **Histological Examination:** Pathologic findings revealed chronic salpingitis in the right oviduct and hydrosalpinx in the left oviduct.
  - **Elimination of Endometriosis:** Absence of lesions during laparoscopy ruled out endometriosis as a cause of the findings.

- **Excluded Diagnoses:**
  - Adenomyosis: Not confirmed during operative inspection.
  - Primary endometrial polyp: Not the dominant pathology in this case but coexisted with other findings.
  - Endometriosis: Laparoscopy did not reveal any lesions.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Intrauterine fallopian tube incarceration, likely resulting from uterine perforation following a vacuum aspiration procedure 4 years prior. The patient exhibited complications including Asherman syndrome, chronic salpingitis, and hydrosalpinx. AUB and secondary infertility were attributed to these findings. Defects were surgically repaired with bilateral salpingectomy and uterine wall suturing.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Intrauterine fallopian tube incarceration with Asherman syndrome and bilateral tubal pathology secondary to uterine perforation after vacuum aspiration.

results.content:
### Resoning:

Step 1: Understanding the Patient's Presenting Issues

The patient is a 40-year-old woman presenting with abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) and secondary infertility for one year. She has a history of placental retention after a vaginal delivery four years ago, which required emergency vacuum curettage. This history is important because it may have led to complications such as uterine adhesions or damage to the uterine lining, which could contribute to her current symptoms.

Step 2: Reviewing the Physical Examination Findings

The physical examination revealed no tenderness or pain in the abdomen and gynecological areas, and the speculum exam did not show active bleeding. This suggests that there may not be an acute pathological process causing the bleeding, such as an infection or a ruptured cyst. However, the absence of tenderness does not rule out chronic conditions that could be causing AUB and infertility.

Step 3: Interpreting the Transvaginal Ultrasound Results

The transvaginal ultrasound showed a hyperechogenic thickened endometrial lining, which was suspected to be an endometrial polyp. Additionally, there were fundal lesions suggestive of adenomyosis and intrauterine adhesions. Endometrial polyps can cause AUB, and adenomyosis is associated with heavy menstrual bleeding and sometimes infertility. Intrauterine adhesions, also known as Asherman's syndrome, can lead to menstrual abnormalities and infertility due to impaired implantation or anatomical distortion of the uterine cavity.

Step 4: Considering the Failed SIS and Hysteroscopy Due to Cervical Stenosis

The failure of saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS) and diagnostic hysteroscopy due to cervical stenosis suggests that there may be significant cervical narrowing, which could be a contributing factor to the patient's symptoms. Cervical stenosis can cause difficulty in menstrual flow, leading to AUB, and can also impair fertility by hindering sperm passage into the uterus.

Step 5: Analyzing the Pelvic MRI Findings

Pelvic MRI provided more detailed information, identifying an endometrial polyp, a nodular subserosa fundic mass communicating with the uterine cavity, left hematosalpinx, and suspected endometriotic lesions. The endometrial polyp confirms the ultrasound findings, and the fundal mass could be related to adenomyosis or another type of uterine pathology. The left hematosalpinx indicates old blood in the fallopian tube, which might be due to a previous inflammation or rupture. Suspected endometriotic lesions could also contribute to infertility and pelvic pain, although the patient does not report chronic pelvic pain.

Step 6: Integrating All Findings to Form a Differential Diagnosis

Given the patient's history, examination findings, and imaging results, several conditions could be contributing to her AUB and infertility:

- Endometrial polyp: Can cause irregular bleeding and may impair fertility.

- Adenomyosis: Associated with heavy menstrual bleeding and may affect fertility.

- Intrauterine adhesions (Asherman's syndrome): Can lead to menstrual abnormalities and infertility.

- Cervical stenosis: May cause obstructive symptoms and impair fertility.

- Hematosalpinx: May indicate tubal pathology that could affect fertility.

- Endometriosis: Can cause infertility and pelvic pain, although the patient does not report pain.

Step 7: Prioritizing the Likely Causes

Given the patient's history of placental retention and the findings of intrauterine adhesions on ultrasound, Asherman's syndrome should be considered as a high-priority diagnosis. The cervical stenosis also needs to be addressed, as it may be exacerbating the bleeding and fertility issues.

Step 8: Considering the Impact on Fertility

The patient has secondary infertility for one year. Conditions such as Asherman's syndrome, adenomyosis, and endometriosis can all impact fertility by altering the uterine environment or causing tubal dysfunction. The presence of intrauterine adhesions can distort the uterine cavity, making it less favorable for embryo implantation.

Step 9: Evaluating the Need for Further Diagnostic Procedures

Given the complexity of the patient's condition and the failed attempts at SIS and hysteroscopy due to cervical stenosis, further diagnostic procedures may be necessary. Options could include hysterosalpingography (HSG), laparoscopy, or perhaps a diagnostic laparoscopy with hysteroscopy under general anesthesia to better visualize the uterine cavity and盆腔情况。

Step 10: Formulating a Management Plan

The management would likely involve a multi-faceted approach:

- Treatment of the endometrial polyp, possibly through hysteroscopic polypectomy once the cervical stenosis is addressed.

- Management of adenomyosis, which may include medical therapy or, in severe cases, surgical intervention.

- Correction of intrauterine adhesions through hysteroscopic adhesiolysis.

- Addressing cervical stenosis, possibly through dilation or other procedures to improve cervical caliber.

- Evaluation and treatment of the hematosalpinx, which may require laparoscopic surgery.

- Consideration of assisted reproductive technologies if other treatments do not resolve the infertility.

Step 11: Considering the Patient's Age and Fertility Desires

The patient is 40 years old and desires to conceive, which adds urgency to the management plan given the age-related decline in fertility. Therefore, expediting diagnostic and therapeutic interventions is important.

Step 12: Synthesizing the Information to Reach a Final Diagnosis

Based on the clinical presentation, imaging findings, and the patient's history, the most likely diagnoses are:

- Intrauterine adhesions (Asherman's syndrome) contributing to AUB and infertility.

- Endometrial polyp causing AUB.

- Adenomyosis possibly contributing to heavy bleeding and infertility.

- Cervical stenosis complicating diagnostic procedures and potentially impacting menstrual flow and fertility.

- Suspected endometriosis based on MRI findings, which may also contribute to infertility.

Given these considerations, the primary focus should be on addressing the intrauterine adhesions and cervical stenosis, followed by management of the endometrial polyp and adenomyosis. Further diagnostic evaluation, such as laparoscopy, may be necessary to fully assess the pelvic environment and confirm the presence of endometriosis or other pathologies.

### Answer:

The patient likely has a combination of intrauterine adhesions (Asherman's syndrome), endometrial polyp, adenomyosis, and cervical stenosis, all of which are contributing to her abnormal uterine bleeding and secondary infertility. Further diagnostic evaluation and a multi-faceted treatment approach are recommended.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Initial differential diagnoses included endometrial polyp, adenomyosis, intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome), uterine perforation with organ incarceration (tubal incarceration), and endometriosis based on imaging findings and clinical observations.

<Step 2> Hysteroscopy and laparoscopy were performed to directly evaluate the uterine cavity and pelvic structures, confirming uterine perforation, fallopian tube incarceration, and intrauterine adhesions.
<Step 3> Histological examination revealed chronic salpingitis in the right oviduct and hydrosalpinx in the left oviduct.
<Step 4> Endometriosis was excluded as a diagnosis due to the absence of lesions during laparoscopy.
<Step 5> Adenomyosis was excluded as it was not confirmed during operative inspection.
<Step 6> Primary endometrial polyp was determined not to be the dominant pathology but was noted to coexist with other findings.
<Step 7> The final diagnosis was intrauterine fallopian tube incarceration, likely resulting from uterine perforation following a vacuum aspiration procedure 4 years prior.
<Step 8> Complications associated with the diagnosis included Asherman syndrome, chronic salpingitis, hydrosalpinx, abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB), and secondary infertility.
<Step 9> Surgical intervention included bilateral salpingectomy and uterine wall suturing to repair the defects.
